Directions
1. Shuck the corn and cut kernels off the cob.
Drain and rinse the butter beans. Dice the red
bell pepper(s). Peel and mince the shallots).
Peel and mince the garlic. Halve and juice the
lemon(s). Slice the radish(es) into thin rounds.
Grate the zucchini(s) on the medium side of
a box grater. Dice the tomato(es).
2. Heat 1 tsp (2 tsp) olive oil in a large nonstick
skillet over medium-high heat. Add corn
kernels and a pinch of salt and cook until

Mix the butter bean succotash.
TIP: We will reuse this skillet in a later step.
3. Add grated zucchini, diced tomato, and
a pinch of salt and pepper to a colander.
Let sit 5 to 6 minutes, and squeeze as much
water out of the mixture as possible. Place
mixture in a medium bowl, and add garbanzo
bean flour and Alamo blend chili seasoning.
Stir to combine.
TIP: To remove moisture from the zucchini and
tomato, use your hands or a spatula to press the
mixture against the colander.
4. Wipe the large nonstick skillet clean, add 1 tbsp
vegetable oil, and heat over medium-high heat.
Scoop 6 portions of tomato zucchini fritter
batter into the skillet, each about ¼ cup. Use
the bottom of a measuring cup bottom to flat-
ten the fritters. Cook fritters until browned and
crisp, 3 to 5 minutes per side. Transfer tomato
zucchini fritters to a paper towel-lined plate
and sprinkle with salt.

Top with tomato zucchini fritters. Garnish

shallots). Mince the bird's eye chile(s).

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Add cashews and ½ cup hot water to a small
bowl, and let nuts soak for at least 10 minutes.
Drain artichoke hearts and pat dry.
2. Heat 1 tbsp olive oil in a large nonstick skillet
over medium heat. Add artichoke hearts
and cook until crispy in places, 5 to 8 minutes.
Transfer crispy artichoke hearts to a plate
and sprinkle with salt.

TIP: We will use this skillet again in a later step.
3. Trim zucchini and slice lengthwise into ¼ inch
thick planks. Stack the zucchini planks and
cut into thin strips to make noodles.
TIP: You can also use a spiralizer or mandoline
to make the zucchini noodles.
4. Zest and halve the lemon, juice one half, and
cut the other half into wedges. Peel the garlic.
Add soaked cashews and the soaking water,
lemon juice, peeled garlic, white miso paste,
nutritional yeast, and a pinch of salt and
pepper to a blender. Blend on high until
cashew cream is smooth.
5. Return skillet to medium-high heat and add
zucchini noodles. Sprinkle with salt and
pepper and cook, tossing occasionally, until
tender, 1 to 2 minutes.
6. Divide zucchini noodles between serving bowls.
Pour cashew cream over noodles and top with
crispy artichoke hearts. Sprinkle with lemon
zest, hemp seeds, pumpkin seeds, and as
much of the red chile flakes as you'd like.
Serve with lemon wedges. Tuck in!

as much of the pith as possible. Peel and thinly
slice the onion(s). Peel and mince the garlic.
Shuck the corn and cut kernels off the cobs).

cheese and bake, uncovered, until peppers are
tender, 20 to 25 minutes.

on the size of your peppers.

Directions
1. Peel and dice the sweet potato(es). Add diced
sweet potato to a small saucepan and cover
with 1 inch water. Bring to a boil and cook until
fork-tender, 10 to 12 minutes. Drain and return
cooked sweet potato to the saucepan, off heat.
Rinse and sort the beluga lentils, and add to a
separate saucepan with 1 cup (2 cups) water.
Bring to a boil, cover, reduce heat to low, and
simmer until lentils are tender, 20 to 24 minutes.
TIP: To "sort" means to look for and discard any
naturally occurring debris or stones.
2. Peel and thinly slice the garlic. Trim, halve, and
deseed the Fresno chile(s), and mince one half
(1 Fresno chile). Peel and mince 2 tsp (4 tsp) gin-
off the broccolini stems.
ger. Halve and juice the lemon(s). Trim 1 inch
TIP: Save the remaining ginger for your own use.
3. Heat 2 tbsp (4 tbsp) olive oil in a large nonstick
skillet over medium heat. Add sliced garlic
and cook until lightly browned, 3 to 5 minutes.
Add minced Fresno chile, minced ginger,
golden raisins, toasted coconut, brown
mustard seeds, and turmeric. Stir and cook
until aromatic, 1 to 3 minutes. Add lemon juice
and transfer the coconut turmeric relish
to a medium bowl.
TIPS: Add more Fresno chile if you prefer more
heat, and save any remaining chile for your own
use. We will use this skillet again in the next step.
4. Wipe the skillet clean and return to medium-
high heat. Add 2 tsp (4 tsp) olive oil, ¼ cup
(½ cup) water, and a pinch of salt. Add brocco-
lini, cover, and cook until water evaporates
and broccolini is tender and crispy in places,
3 to 5 minutes. Flip and cook for an additional
2 minutes.

5. Add butter to the cooked sweet potatoes
and mash with a fork. Taste, and add salt as
necessary. Divide the mashed sweet potatoes
and beluga lentils between large plates.

Aioli vs Mayo—What's the Difference?
Both have a creamy consistency and whitish
• vitamin-packed base with cauliflower steaks alu LEOLy IUILiN
sauce. Bright, fresh vegetables give the dish a pop of color
color. But mayonnaise is an emulsion made
with egg yolk and oil, and aioli is an emulsion
created with just garlic and extra virgin olive oil.
Sometimes egg yolk is added as well, but even
then, the addition of garlic sets aioli apart from
mayo. In this recipe we put our own spin on
things by making lime aioli, which includes
minced garlic, lime zest, lime juice, Vegenaise,
and a pinch of salt. It's so good!

SERVING

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Add garbanzo bean flour, curry powder, and
½ cup cold water to a large bowl and whisk to
combine. Grate carrots using the large side of
a box grater. Add 2 cups grated carrot, ½ tsp
salt and ¼ tsp pepper to the large bowl with the
batter. Mix the carrot batter and set aside.
2. Peel and mince the shallot. Peel and mince
the garlic. Peel the mango, remove the pit,
and thinly slice the flesh. Halve the avocado,
remove the pit, and thinly slice the flesh. Halve
the grape tomatoes. Pick and roughly chop the
parsley leaves. Zest, halve, and juice the lime.
3. Add minced shallot, minced garlic, sliced
mango, sliced avocado, halved tomatoes,
chopped parsley leaves, lime zest, lime juice,
½ tsp salt, and a pinch of pepper to a large
bowl. Toss the mango avocado salad and
refrigerate until ready to serve.
4. Heat 1 tbsp vegetable oil in a large nonstick
skillet over medium-high heat. Scoop 6 portions
of carrot batter into the skillet, each about
¼ cup. Use a measuring cup bottom to flatten
the fritters, and cook fritters until browned and
crisp, 3 to 5 minutes per side. Transfer curried
carrot fritters to a paper towel-lined plate and
sprinkle with salt.
TIP: If necessary, work in batches to avoid
overcrowding the skillet.
5. Divide the curried carrot fritters between
plates and drizzle with tamarind chutney.
Serve with mango avocado salad. Dig in!
Madras-Style Curry Powder:
Pure Delight
Madras curry originated in southeastern India
• vitamin-packed base with caulitlower steaks anu toly utiN
sauce. Briaht, fresh vegetables give the dish a pop of color
and is well known for its warm, aromatic quality.
Our spice blend recreates that essence with a
sophisticated mix of turmeric, cumin, coriander,
and fenugreek, adding tons of flavor to your meal.

Crispy Seitan = Amazing Seitan
We love seitan because it's high in protein, mim-
ics the texture of meat, and impressively soaks up
flavor. And while there are many ways to serve it,
in this recipe it's all about creating some crisp.
To make delightfully crispy seitan, get the pan

and high heat-and make sure you're using
enough oil. Finally, toss the seitan occasionally,

Directions
1. Preheat the oven to 400°F. Crumble the tofu
into bite-size peces. Peel and mince the
shallot(s) and garlic. Drain the red
peppers. Pick the mint leaves from the stems.
2. Place the crumbled tofu on a baking sheet
and toss with 2 tsp (4 tsp) olive oil, za'atar
seasoning, and a pinch of salt and pepper.
Bake until browned in places, 12 to 15 minutes.

3. Heat 3 tbsp (6 tbsp) olive oil in a large skillet

Cook until fragrant, 1 to 2 minutes. Add the red
peppers, tomatoes, ½ tsp (1 tsp) salt, and a
pinch of pepper. Bring to asimmer and cook
until the sauce hickens, 5 to 8 minutes.
4. Place the multigrain flatbread(s) directly on
the oven rack. Bake until slightly crisp,
3 to 5 minutes. Add the baked za'atar tofu to

the roasted red pepper shakshuka from
heat and dollop with cashew cheese. Top with
crispy shallots and mint leaves. Serve family
style with crispy flatbread on the side. Enjoy!
What is shakshuka?
Though it originated in Northern Africa,
Shakshuka has been embraced by cultures
around the world, particularly in Israel where
it's one of the country's national dishes. Tradi-
tionally, eggs are poached in a smoky, spicy,
vegetable-packed tomato sauce, but in this recipe
we replace eggs with cashew cheese. The result
is a tasty protein source. Ultimately, no matter
which ingredients you include, this one-skillet
wonder is a delicious meal that can be served for
breakfast, lunch, or dinner!

and a delicious crunch.

ocr.fullText:
Birections
1. Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil.
Peel and thinly slice the garlic. Trim the yellow
squash and slice into half moons. Halve the
lemon(s), juice one half (1 lemon), and divide
the other half (1 lemon) into wedges. Pick the
basil leaves from the stems.
2. Heat 2 tsp (4 tsp) olive oil in a large nonstick
skillet over medium-high heat. Add the sliced
garlic and a pinch of salt and pepper. Cook
until garlic is lightly browned, 2 to 3 minutes.
Transfer the crispy garlic to a small bowl and
return skillet to medium-high heat.
3. Add the sliced yellow squash to the skillet
and cook until lightly browned in places,
3 to 4 minutes. Reduce heat to low and cook
until the squash softens and breaks down,
summer squash to a bowl.
another 5 to 7 minutes. Transfer the melted
TIP: We will reuse this skillet in a later step.
4. Add the gnocchi to the large pot of boiling
water and cook until the gnocchi floats, 3 to 5
minutes. Drain the cooked gnocchi and shake
to remove excess water.
5. Return skillet to medium-high heat with the
butter. Once melted, add the cooked gnocchi
and cook, undisturbed, until browned in places,
2 to 4 minutes. Add the parmesan and melted
summer squash and stir to combine. Remove
skillet from heat and add the lemon juice.
6. Divide the parmesan gnocchi with melted
summer squash between bowls. Top with

wedges. Dig in!

occasionally, until nuts are toasted, 3 to 5 min-
utes. Let toasted hazelnuts cool, and transfer
to a cutting board. Remove skins and roughly
chop the nuts. Add chopped tarragon, minced
garlic, chopped hazelnuts, 2 tbsp olive oil,
and a pinch of salt to a small bowl. Stir the
tarragon hazelnut relish.
4. Return the skillet to medium-heat with 1 tbsp
olive oil. Add liced broccoli florets and cook
until until crisp-tender, 2 to 3 minutes per side.
Add chopped broccoli rabe and cook until
bright green and just wilted, 3 to 5 minutes.
Sprinkle charred broccoli with salt and add just
half the lemon juice.
5. Fluff the cooked millet with a fork. Add remain-
ing lemon juice, capers, golden raisins, and a
pinch of salt, and stir. Divide the lemon caper
millet between plates. Top with Sicilian-style
broccoli and tarragon hazelnut relish. Tuck in!
• vitamin-packed base with cauliflower steaks and zesty tzatzikı
sauce. Bright, fresh egetables give the dish a pop of color
and a delicious crunch.
Culinary Inspo? Caponata!
When creating this dish our culinary team was
inspired by caponata—described by Martha Rose
Shulman, a food columnist for The New York
Times, as "a Sicilian sweet and sour version of
ratatouille." It's sweet, savory, and a little nutty,
just like this dish. Enjoy!

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Peel and dice the sweet potato(es). Add diced
sweet potato to a small saucepan and cover
with 1 inch water. Bring to a boil and cook until
fork-tender, 10 to 12 minutes. Drain and return
cooked sweet potato to the saucepan, off heat.
Rinse and sort the beluga lentils, and add to a
separate saucepan with 1 cup (2 cups) water.
Bring to a boil, cover, reduce heat to low, and
simmer until lentils are tender, 20 to 24 minutes.
TIP: To "sort" means to look for and discard any
naturally ocurring debris or stones.
2. Peel and thinly slice the garlic. Trim, halve, and
deseed the Fresno chile(s), and mince one half
(1 Fresno chile). Peel and mince 2 tsp (4 tsp)
ginger. Halve and juice the lemon(s). Trim
1 inch off the broccolini stems.
TIP: Save the remining ginger for your own use.
3. Heat 2 tbsp (4 tbsp) olive oil in a large nonstick
skillet over medium heat. Add sliced garlic and
cook until lightly browned, 3 to 5 minutes. Add
minced Fresno chile, minced ginger, golden
raisins, coconut, brown mustard seeds, and
turmeric. Stir and cook until aromatic, 1 to 3
minutes. Add lemon juice and transfer the
coconut turmeric relish to a medium bowl.
TIP: Add more Fresno chile if you prefer more heat.
Save any remaining chile for your own use.
4. Wipe the skillet clean and return to medi-
um-high heat. Add 2 tsp (4 tsp) olive oil, ¼ cup
(½ cup) water, and a pinch of salt. Add
broccolini, cover, and cook until water
evaporates and broccolini is tender and crispy
in places, 3 to 5 minutes. Flip and cook for
an additional 2 minutes.
5. Add butter to the cooked sweet potatoes and
vitamin-packed base with cauliflower steaks and zesty zatzikı
sauce. Bright, fresh egetables give the dish a pop of color
mash with a fork. Taste, and add salt as neces-
sary. Divide the mashed sweet potatoes and
cooked beluga lentils between large plates.
Top with pan seared broccolini and coconut
turmeric relish. Enjoy!

SERVING

2. Add diced russet potato, red lentils, 1¼ cups

fork-tender, 14 to 16 minutes. Mash the red
lentil skordalia with a fork.
3. Line a baking sheet with foil or parchment

over medium heat. Add green beans and cook
until they are charred in places, 5 to 8 minutes.

Birections
1. Peel and dice the onion. Trim, deseed, and dice
the green bell pepper. Dice the celery. Peel
and mince the garlic. Desteem the curly kale
and roughly chop the leaves.
2. Remove sausages from the plastic casing and
slice into rounds. Heat 2 tbsp vegetable oil in
a large pot over medium-high heat. Add liced
sausage and cook, stirring occasionally, until it
Tag your best Purple Carrot pics with
#nurnlecarrot for a chance to be
begins to crisp, 4 to 5 minutes. Add chopped
curly kale and cook until kale is bright green
and wilted, 1 to 2 minutes. Transfer sautéed
sausage and curly kale to a plate.
3. Return the pot to medium-high heat. Add diced
onion, diced green bell pepper, and diced
celery to the pot and cook until softened, 3 to 4
minutes. Add minced garlic and a pinch of salt
and cook until fragrant, about 30 seconds.
4. Add the basmati rice, chipotle morita powder,
tomatoes, vegetable broth concentrate, and
1¼ cups water to the pot. Bring to a boil, cover,
reduce heat to low, and cook until the water is
absorbed and rice is tender, 15 to 18 minutes.
TIP: If you're sensitive to spice, omit or use
less chipotle.
5. Add the sautéed sausage and curly kale to the
jambalaya and cook until warm, 1 to 2 minutes.
Divide the sausage jambalaya with kale and
long-grain rice between large bowls. Dig in!
A Divine and Delicious Base
You've probably heard of mirepoix, the French
term for a flavor base made from sautéed onion,
carrot, and celery. It's a key component of many
of the soups, stews, and sauces you love. But say
you swap in green peppers for carrots, what's
• vitamin-packed base with cauliflower steaks and zesty tzatzikı
sauce. Bright, fresh egetables give the dish a pop of color
and a delicious crunch.
that called? In the southeastern United States,
people call that mixture the Holy Trinity, and it's
the fondation of classic Cajun dishes like gumbo
and jambalaya.

Let's Talk about Lupini Beans
Lupini beans, a legume related to chickpeas and
lentils, are popular in Mediterranean cisines. Be-
cause they have a slightly bitter taste, these beans
are often pickled and eaten as a snack. They have
a thick skin that you can eat, or you can squeeze
the lupini bean and the seed will shoot out!

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Preheat the oven to 375°F. Rinse and sort the
French green lentils, and add to a small
saucepan with 2¼ cups water and a pinch of
salt. Bring to a boil, cover, reduce heat to low,
and simmer until lentils are tender, 18 to 20
minutes. Drain any remaining water.
TIP: To "sort" means to look for and discard any
naturally occurring debris or stones.
Tag your best Purple Carrot pics with
#purplecarrot for a chance to be
2. Trim the ends off the delicata squash, remove
the seeds, and slice squash into 6 rounds, each
about ½½ inch thick. Add sliced squash to a bak-
ing sheet and toss with 1 tbsp vegetable oil and
a pinch of salt and pepper. Roast until
fork-tender, 18 to 20 minutes.
3. Zest and halve the lemon, juice one half, and
cut the other half into wedges. Add yogurt, just
½ tsp lemon zest, just 2 tsp lemon juice, and a
pinch of salt to a small bowl, and stir. In another
small bowl, combine butter and as much chile
garlic sauce as you'd like, and mix well.
4. Peel and thinly slice the garlic. Roughly chop the
gai lan. Heat 2 tsp vegetable oil in a large non-
stick skillet over medium-high heat. Add liced
garlic and cook until lightly browned in places.
Add chopped gai lan and a pinch of salt and
pepper and cook until tender, 2 to 3 minutes.
5. Pick the dill fonds from the stem. Spread
chile butter on the roasted squash. Divide the
French green lentils and garlicky greens be-
tween plates, and top with chile butter roasted
delicata squash. Drizzle with lemon yogurt
and sprinkle with dill fronds and remaining
lemon zest. Serve with lemon wedges and dol-
lop any remaining chile butter on top. Enjoy!
TIP: The dill fonds are the wispy green leaves
• vitamin-packed base with cauliflower steaks and zesty Izalzikı
sauce. Bright, fresh egetables give the dish a pop of color
and a delicious crunch.
on the thick stems.
Delicate and Delicious!
Some people say delicata squash tastes like a mix
of fresh corn and pumpkin. We say: Yum! Plus,
delicata squash is high in beta-carotene and
vitamin C, low in calories, and astonishingly
versatile. It's great boiled, sautéed, steamed, and
NUTRITION PER
SERVING
roasted—and the skin is delicious roasted, too.

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Preheat oven to 400°F. Add the quinoa, ½ cup (1
cup) water, and a pinch of salt to a small sauce-
pan over high heat. Bring to a boil, reduce heat,
cover, and cook until the spirals burst and water
is absorbed, 10 to 12 minutes.
2. Transfer the cauliflower florets to a baking
sheet and toss with 1 tbsp (2 tbsp) olive oil and
a pinch of salt and pepper. Roast until tender
and browned in places, 12 to 15 minutes.
3. Peel and chop the garlic. Peel and roughly chop
the shallot(s). Heat 1 tbsp (2 tbsp) olive oil in
a large pot over medium heat. Add chopped
garlic and chopped shallot and cook until
softened, 3 to 5 minutes.
4. Add the cashews, tomatoes, coconut milk,
and ¼ cup (½ cup) water to the large pot.
Bring to a boil, reduce heat to low, and simmer
for 3 to 5 minutes. Carefully pour bisque into
a blender and purée until smooth. Sprinkle
tomato bisque with salt and pepper.
TIP: Blend in batches for the 4 serving meal.
5. Tear the basil leaves. Toss roasted
cauliflower with just 1 tbsp (2 tbsp) parmesan.
Divide the creamy tomato bisque between
large bowls. Top with quinoa and parmesan
roasted cauliflower. Sprinkle with torn basil
leaves and remaining parmesan. Soup's on!
Avoid a Soup Explosion
Hot liquids expand and can create a powerful
vortex in your blender. Follow these tips to
ensure your soup ends up in your bowls, not
on your walls.
Give the soup miture a few minutes to cool
down. Fill half the blender jar with soup, add the
bender lid, and remove the center part of the lid

sauce. Bright, fresh egetables give the dish a pop of color
and a delicious crunch.
or open the spout to release steam. Hold a towel
over the opening to prevent soup from escaping,
and turn on the blender. Start on low and slowly
increase the speed. Voilà!

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Preheat the oven to 450°F. Peel and roughly
chop the carrots and onion(s). Trim and halve
the Brussels sprouts. Halve the fingerling
potatoes lengthwise. Remove sausages from
the casing and halve lengthwise.
2. Add the chopped carrot, chopped onion,
halved Brussels sprouts, halved fingerling
potatoes, caraway seeds, 1 tbsp (2 tbsp) olive
oil, and a pinch of salt and pepper to a baking
O+
sheet. Toss to evenly coat, and roast until the
vegetables begin to brown, 15 to 17 minutes.
3. Combine the Dijon mustard and maple
syrup in a small bowl. Mix the maple
mustard dressing.
4. Heat 1 tbsp (2 tbsp) olive oil in a large nonstick
skillet over medium-high heat. Place halved
sausages cut side down and cook until well
browned and crisp, 3 to 5 minutes. Flip sausages
and cook another 1 to 3 minutes.
5. Divide the caraway roasted vegetables and
herbed sausages between large, shallow plates.
Drizzle 1 tbsp (2 tbsp) maple mustard dressing
on top, and serve any remaning dressing on the
side for dipping. Enjoy!
Caraway Power!
If you've eaten rye or pumpernickel breads, you've
tasted caraway seeds. These delicious seeds,
which are part of the parsley family, add a mild
anise flavor and a citrusy, peppery smell to all
sorts of classic dishes and savory baked goods.
In particular, caraway is very common in Aus-
trian and German cooking, but you'll also find it
in Middle Eastern dishes and in condiments like
harissa.
Besides being a popular culinary ingredient,
• vitamin-packed base with cauliflower steaks and zesty tzaLiKI
sauce. Bright, fresh vegetables give the dish a pop of color
caraway has been used for hundreds of years as a
digestive aid. Research shows that these flavorful
seeds can help rebuild your intestinal flora and
yeast, acting as a natural probiotic.

1. Peel and dice the sweet potato. Add diced
sweet potato to a small saucepan and cover
with 1 inch of water. Bring to a boil and cook

return cooked sweet potato to the saucepan,
off heat. Add beluga lentils and 1 cup water to
another saucepan. Bring to a boil, cover, reduce
20 to 24 minutes.

3. Heat 2 tbsp olive oil in a large nonstick skil-
let over medium heat. Add sliced garlic and
cook until lightly browned, 3 to 5 minutes. Add
minced Fresno chile, minced ginger, golden
raisins, coconut, brown mustard seeds, and
turmeric. Stir, and cook until aromatic, 1 to 3 °
minutes. Add lemon juice and transfer the
coconut turmeric relish to a medium bowl.

cook for an additional 2 minutes.

mash with a fork. Taste, and add salt as neces-
sary. Divide the mashed sweet potatoes and
cooked beluga lentils between large plates.

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil for
the pasta. Thinly slice the cremini mushrooms.
Peel and mince the garlic and shallots). Add
walnuts to a large nonstick skillet over medi-
um heat and toast, shaking the pan frequently,
until fragrant, 3 to 5 minutes. Transfer toasted
walnuts to a plate.
TIP: Don't forget to wash all produce under running
water, and wipe mushrooms clean with a damp
kitchen towel.
0.0
2. Return the skillet to medium-high heat with
1 tbsp (2 tbsp) olive oil. Once hot, add sliced
cremini mushrooms and a pinch of salt and
cook, tossing occasionally, 3 to 5 minutes.
Add minced garlic, minced shallot, and a pinch
of salt and pepper, and cook until softened,
2 to 4 minutes.
3. Add pasta to the boiling water. Cook, stirring
occasionally, until al dente, 3 to 5 minutes.
Reserve 1 cup (2 cups) pasta water. Drain the
pasta and rinse under cold water to stop the
cooking process.
4. Add the butter and sherry wine to the skillet
with the mushrooms and cook for 1 minute.
Add bouillon cube(s) to the reserved pasta
water, whisk, and add to the skillet. Bring to
a boil and cook until slightly thickened, 2 to 3
minutes. Add baby spinach and cooked pasta
to the skillet and cook until hot, 1 to 2 minutes.
5. Divide the penne mushroom florentine
between large bowls and top with toasted
walnuts. Buon appetito!
Delicious, Healthy, and... Foamy?
Chickpea pasta is gluten-free and packed with
protein and fiber, plus it has fewer carbs than
vitamin-packed base with cauliflower steaks and zesty tzatziki
sauce. Bright, fresh vegetables give the dish a pop of color
traditional pasta. This is definitely the vegan
pasta we've been waiting for.
BTW, it's totally normal to see foam when you
boil chickpea pasta. To help reduce the foaming,
fill your pot with more water than you need, and
lower the heat. You can also add a splash of oil,
and frequently stir the pasta.

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Add 2 quarts water and 1 tbsp salt to a medium
saucepan and bring to a boil. Chop gai lan into
1 inch pieces. Peel and mince 2 tsp ginger. Trim
and thinly slice the mini sweet peppers.
TIP: Save any remaining ginger for your own use.
2. Add the minced ginger, tamari, peanut
butter, rice vinegar, just half the sesame oil,
just half the chili garlic sauce, and ¼ hot
water to a medium bowl and whisk the sesame
ginger sauce.
TIPS: The sauce will not be completely smooth. Keep
any remaining chili garlic sauce for your own use.
3. Add the udon to the boiling water and cook until
al dente, 6 to 8 minutes. Drain noodles and run
under cold water to stop the cooking process.
Heat the remaining sesame oil in a large non-
stick skillet over medium heat. Add peanuts
and a pinch of salt and pepper. Cook until nuts
are toasted and fragrant, 1 to 3 minutes. Use a
slotted spoon to transfer the toasted peanuts
to a small bowl, leaving the oil in the skillet.
4. Return the skillet with sesame oil to high heat.
Thinly slice the Swiss chard. Add chopped gai
lan, sliced Swiss chard, and a pinch of salt and
pepper to the skillet. Cook until bright green
and tender, 2 to 3 minutes.
5. Add the cooked udon and sesame ginger
sauce to the skillet with the greens. Cook, toss-
ing frequently, until evenly coated and warmed
through, 1 to 3 minutes.
6. Divide the sesame ginger noodles and
stir-fried greens between bowls. Top with
sliced mini sweet peppers and toasted
peanuts. Enjoy!

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Rinse and sort the beluga lentils, and add to
a small saucepan with ½ cup water. Bring to a
boil, reduce heat to low, and simmer, covered,
until lentils are tender, 20 to 24 minutes. Drain
any remaining water.
TIP: To "sort" means to look for and discard any
naturally occurring debris or stones.
2. Add the red bliss potatoes to a large pot, cover
with 1 inch cold water, and bring to a boil. Cook
until potatoes are fork-tender, 10 to 12 minutes.
Drain and let cool.
3. Thinly slice the sugar snap peas and scallions.
Halve and juice the lemon. Add sliced snap
peas, just half the sliced scallion, lemon juice,
1 tbsp olive oil, and a pinch of salt to a medium
bowl. Toss the snap pea slaw and set aside.
Slice the red bell pepper into thin strips. Thinly
slice the jalapeño.
4. Add remaining sliced scallion, sour cream
and a pinch of salt to a small bowl. Stir to
combine the scallion sour cream. Peel and
mince the garlic.
5. Place cooled boiled potatoes on a cutting
board and, using the palm of your hand, gently
press each potato until the sides blister open.
Heat 1 tbsp vegetable oil in a large skillet over
medium heat. Add smashed potatoes and cook
until golden brown and crispy, 3 to 5 minutes
per side. Add sliced red bell pepper, minced
garlic, butter, and paprika, and cook another
2 to 3 minutes.
6. Taste the crispy potato skillet and add salt as
necessary. Scatter cooked beluga lentils over
the skillet. Serve family style, topped with snap
pea slaw, scallion sour cream, and sliced
jalapeño. Bon appétit!

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil for
the udon. Wipe cremini mushrooms clean with
a damp kitchen towel. Roughly chop the
cremini mushrooms. Peel and mince the garlic
and shallot. Trim 1 inch off the gai lan ends.
Thinly slice the cucumber into rounds, and add
sliced cucumber to a small bowl with just
1 tbsp chile garlic sauce.
2. Add the remaining chile garlic sauce, tahini,
rice vinegar, tamari, and ⅓ cup hot water to
a medium bowl and mix.
3. Add the udon to the boiling water and gently
stir. Cook until al dente, 5 to 7 minutes.
Drain and run under cold water to stop the
cooking process.
4. Heat 2 tsp vegetable oil in a large nonstick skil-
let over medium-high heat. Add the gai lan and
a pinch of salt and pepper and cook until gai
lan is bright green and tender, 3 to 4 minutes.
Transfer the sautéed gai lan to a plate and
cover to keep warm.
5. Return the skillet to medium-high heat and add
2 tbsp vegetable oil. Add chopped cremini
mushrooms, minced garlic, minced shallot,
and a pinch of salt and pepper. Cook until
the mushrooms are browned, 4 to 5 minutes.
Remove from heat, add the cooked udon and
sauce to the skillet, and toss.
6. Halve the lemon and divide one half into
wedges. Divide the dan dan noodles between
plates and serve with the gai lan and spicy cu-
cumbers. Sprinkle with smoked nori spice and
squeeze the lemon half over each plate. Serve
with lemon wedges. Enjoy!

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Preheat the oven to 425°F. Dice sweet
potato(es) into 1 inch pieces. Peel and chop the
red onion(s) into 1 inch pieces. Transfer diced
sweet potato and chopped red onion to
a baking sheet and toss with 1 tbsp (2 tbsp)
vegetable oil, cumin seeds, and a pinch of salt
and pepper. Roast until sweet potatoes are
tender, 18 to 22 minutes.
0.0
Tag your best Purple Carrot pics with
2. Peel and mince the garlic. Finely chop the
cilantro leaves and stems. Halve and juice
the lime(s). Add minced garlic, chopped
cilantro, lime juice, Vegenaise, and a pinch
of salt to a small bowl and mix the cilantro
lime dressing
3. Slice the corn tortillas into thin ¼ inch thick
strips. Place tortilla strips on a baking sheet
and toss with 2 tsp (4 tsp) vegetable oil and
a pinch of salt. Bake until crispy and lightly
browned, 5 to 6 minutes.
TIP: Toss the tortilla strips halfway through baking.
4. Drain and rinse the black beans. Trim and chop
the baby romaine lettuce. Trim and thinly slice
the radishes. Halve the avocado(s), remove the
pit(s), and dice the flesh.
5. Divide the chopped baby romaine lettuce
between large plates and top with the black
beans, roasted vegetables, sliced radish, and
diced avocado. Drizzle Mexican Cobb salads
with cilantro lime dressing and sprinkle with
crispy tortilla strips. Dig in!
The Most Polarizing Herb?
Cilantro has been around for centuries. In fact,
evidence of cilantro seeds was found in King Tut's
tomb! Today you can find cilantro in Middle East-
• vitamin-packed base with cauliflower steaks and zesty tzatziki
sauce. Bright, fresh vegetables give the dish a pop of color
and a delicious crunch.
ern, North African, European, and Asian cuisines.
But despite cilantro's inclusion in so many meals,
its taste is very controversial; people tend to
either love it or hate it. For those of us who love
it, we can't get enough of the fresh, citrusy taste.
However, for about 20 percent of the population
cilantro will taste like soap. Why? It's a genetic
predisposition. Fun fact: Julia Child famously
despised cilantro.

Directions
1. Preheat the oven to 425°F. Slice Yukon Gold
potatoes into ½ inch thick wedges and place on
a baking sheet. Toss with 2 tsp (4 tsp) vegetable
oil, a pinch of salt, and ¼ tsp (½ tsp) pepper.
Roast potatoes wedges until crisp in places and
lightly browned, 20 to 25 minutes.
2. Drain the hearts of palm and pat dry with
a clean kitchen towel. Transfer to a baking sheet
0.0
and gently toss with 1 tsp (2 tsp) vegetable
oil, New England seafood seasoning, and
a pinch of salt and pepper. Place pieces cut
18 to 20 minutes.
side down and roast until lightly browned,
3. Peel and mince the shallots). Trim and
thinly slice the celery. Finely chop dill fronds.
Zest and halve the lemon(s), juice one half
(1 lemon), and divide the other half (1 lemon)
into wedges. Add minced shallot, sliced celery,
chopped dill, just 1 tbsp (2 tbsp) lemon juice,
Vegenaise, and capers to a large bowl. Mix the
dill dressing. Taste, and add salt and pepper
3
as necessary.
4. Place a large nonstick skillet over medium-high
heat. Brush the top of each hot dog bun with
1 tsp olive oil and place oiled-side down in the
skillet to toast, 1 to 2 minutes.
TIP: You can also toast hot dog buns in the oven.
5. Add lemon zest to the roasted potato wedges
and toss to coat. Add roasted hearts of palm
to the dill dressing and gently toss until evenly
coated. Divide mixture between the toasted hot
dog buns. Serve the New England lobster rolls
with lemon pepper potato wedges and lemon
wedges on the side. Bon appétit!
Sea Seasoning
• vitamin-packed base with cauliflower steaks and zesty tzatziki
sauce. Bright, fresh vegetables give the dish a pop of color
and a delicious crunch.
Even when you're eating plant-based meals,
there's just something about the allure of foods
from the sea—especially in the summer! Our
famous lobster rolls transform hearts of palm
into something truly spectacular, with some help
from New England seasoning. Made with salt,
paprika, garlic, lemon peel, onion powder, red
pepper, bay leaves, and mustard, this seasoning
is light, sweet, and a little bit briny. Yum!

2. Destem the kale and thinly slice the leaves.

whisk together the minced garlic, tahini, tamari,
agave, sesame oil, just 1 tbsp rice vinegar, 2 tbsp
hot water, and a pinch of salt. Mix the tahini

3. Heat a large nonstick skillet over medium-high
heat with 1 tbsp vegetable oil. Add the sliced
mushrooms and cook, stirring occasionally, until

4. Once the water is boiling, add the soba noodles
and gently stir. Cook until al dente, 2 to 3 minutes.
Drain and run under cool water to stop the
cooking process.
5. Return the skillet to medium-high heat and add
1 tsp vegetable oil and the sliced kale. Cook until
wilted, 2 to 3 minutes. Remove the skillet from
heat and add the cooked soba noodles, just ¼ cup
of the cooked lentils, and the tahini sauce, and
stir well.
6. Toss the pea shoots with the remaining rice
vinegar and a pinch of salt, directly on the cutting
board. Divide the creamy tahini noodles between
large bowls and add the crispy shiitakes. Sprinkle
with the remaining beluga lentils and pea shoots.

4. Pick the mint leaves. Halve and juice the
lime(s). Remove ginger from the broth and add
sliced baby bok choy. Add ½ tsp (1 tsp) salt
and let broth continue to simmer for about
1 minute. Remove saucepan from heat and add
the lime juice.
5. Divide cooked udon and carrot "noodles"
between bowls and top with spicy red curry
coconut broth. Sprinkle with mint leaves.
Enjoy!
Red Curry Paste
Curry paste is made of fresh ingredients that are
ground together to create a thick pureé. Pastes
are usually thinned with a liquid, like coconut
milk, to create soups or sauces. In this red curry
paste we combine dried red chilies, garlic,
shallot, salt, lemongrass, sugar, makrut lime,

galangal, and spices like coriander seeds, cumin,
and cardamom. The flavor tends to be a bit less
spicy-the paste contains fewer red chilies-but
it definitely maintains a tasty, moderate spice
level, with some sweetness.

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Bring a large pot of salted water to boil for
the udon. Trim the gai lan, roughly chop
the leaves, and thinly slice the stems. Slice
yellow squash(es) into rounds. Thinly slice the
scallions. Peel the garlic. Halve the lime(s),
juice one half (1 lime), and cut the other half
(1 lime) into wedges.
2. Place a large nonstick skillet over medium-low
heat and add all the peanuts. Toast until fra-
Follow us on social @purplecarrotxo
grant, 3 to 4 minutes, and transfer to a bowl.
Add garlic, lime juice, almond butter, tamari,
turbinado sugar, chile garlic sauce, ½ cup
(1 cup) water, and a pinch of salt to a blend-
er. Blend almond butter sauce on high until
smooth. Add just ¼ cup (½ cup) toasted
peanuts and pulse to combine.
TIP: The peanuts are added last, and pulsed instead
of blended, to give the sauce texture.
3. Once the water is boiling, add udon and gently
stir. Cook until al dente, 6 to 8 minutes. Drain
udon and run under cold water to stop the
cooking process.
4. Return skillet to medium-high heat with 2 tsp
(4 tsp) vegetable oil. Add chopped gai lan
leaves and sliced gai lan stems, sliced yellow
squash, and a pinch of salt. Cook until gai lan
is bright green and tender, 3 to 5 minutes.
Reduce heat to low, add spicy almond butter
sauce, and bring to a simmer.
5. Add the cooked udon and half the sliced scal-
lions to the skillet with the vegetables, and stir.
Taste, and add salt as necessary. Divide spicy
almond butter noodles between bowls and top
with remaining toasted peanuts and remaining
sliced scallions. Serve with lime wedges. Dig in!
While it's traditionally eaten with pita, you'll instead pair this
vitamin-packed base with cauliflower steaks and zesty tzatziki
sauce. Bright, fresh vegetables give the dish a pop of color
Get to Know Gai Lan
Gai lan, or Chinese broccoli, is a nutrient-rich
veggie. It's full of calcium, vitamin K, potassium,
vitamin A, fiber, and folate. Gai lan is used
a good deal in Chinese cooking, but it's also
prevalent in Korean, Vietnamese, and Thai dishes.
Fun fact: When you combine gai lan and broccoli
you get broccolini.

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil for
the gnocchi. Cut green beans in half. Trim
the zucchini(s) and peel lengthwise to make
ribbons. Halve the lemon(s), juice one half
(1 lemon), and cut the other half (1 lemon)
into wedges.
2. Add the zucchini ribbons, lemon juice, Aleppo
pepper flakes, and a pinch of salt to a medium
bowl. Toss to combine.
Follow us on social @purplecarrotxo
00
Tag your best Purple Carrot pics with
3. Heat 2 tsp (4 tsp) olive oil in a large nonstick
skillet over medium-high heat. Add halved
green beans and cook until browned in places,
3 to 4 minutes. Add ¼ cup (½ cup) water and
reduce heat to low. Cook green beans until
crisp-tender, another 2 to 3 minutes. Sprinkle
charred green beans with salt and pepper,
and transfer to a bowl.
TIP: We will reuse this skillet in a later step.
4. Add the gnocchi to the large pot of boiling water
and cook until they float to the surface,
3 to 5 minutes. Drain gnocchi and shake off any
excess water.
5. Return skillet to medium heat with 1 tbsp
(2 tbsp) olive oil. Add cooked gnocchi and
sauté, undisturbed, until browned in places,
2 to 4 minutes. Return charred green beans
to the skillet along with the basil pesto and toss
to combine. Sprinkle gnocchi with salt
and pepper.
TIP: If the pesto is too thick, add 1 tablespoon
warm water (or more, if necessary), to create
a thinner consistency.
6. Divide the gnocchi al pesto with charred green
beans between shallow bowls. Serve with
lemon zucchini and lemon wedges. Mangia!
While it's traditionally eaten with pita, you'll instead pair this
vitamin-packed base with cauliflower steaks and zesty tzatziki
sauce. Bright, fresh vegetables give the dish a pop of color
and delicious crunch.

ocr.fullText:
Directions
1. Bring a large pot with 4 quarts water and 2 tbsp
salt to a boil. Peel and mince the garlic and
shallot. Peel and dice the carrots. Trim 1 inch
off the asparagus and cut the remaining stalks
into rounds. Thinly slice the basil. Zest, halve,
and juice the lemon. Drain and rinse the
butter beans.
2. Heat 1 tbsp olive oil in a large nonstick skillet
over medium-high heat. Add the minced garlic,
Follow us on social @purplecarrotxo
minced shallot, diced carrot, and a pinch of salt
and pepper. Cook, stirring often, until the carrots
are tender, 5 to 6 minutes.
3. Add the gnocchi to the large pot of boiling water
and cook until they float to the surface, 3 to 5
minutes. Reserve ½ cup of the pasta water and
drain the gnocchi. Shake off any excess water.
4. Return the skillet with the carrots to medium-high
heat and add the pasta water and butter and
cook until melted, 1 to 2 minutes. Add the
asparagus rounds and cook until bright green,
2 to 3 minutes. Add the lemon juice and just half
of the basil, taste, and add salt and pepper as
necessary. Transfer the spring vegetables and
lemon basil butter to a medium bowl and cover
to keep warm.
5. Wipe the skillet and return to high heat with
1 tbsp olive oil. Once hot, add the butter beans,
cooked gnocchi, ½ tsp salt, and ¼ tsp pepper.
Cook, undisturbed, until well browned, 3 to 4
minutes. Sprinkle with lemon zest and parmesan,
toss, and cook for another 2 minutes.
6. Divide the spring vegetables and lemon basil
butter between bowls and top with the gnocchi
and butter beans. Sprinkle with the remaining
sliced basil. Enjoy!
While it's traditionally eaten with pita, you'll instead pair this
• vitamin-packed base with cauliflower steaks and zesty tzatziki
sauce. Bright, fresh vegetables give the dish a pop of color

Directions
1. Preheat oven to 425°F. Trim the eggplant and cut
lengthwise into 4 planks. Place on a baking sheet
and toss with 1 tbsp olive oil and a pinch of salt
and pepper. Bake until lightly browned on each
side, flipping halfway through, 15 to 20 minutes.
2. Peel and mince the shallot and garlic. Zest and

halve the lemon; juice one half and cut the other

breadcrumbs, and 2 tsp olive oil to a small bowl.
Toss to combine.
3. Heat a medium saucepan over medium-high heat
with 1 tbsp olive oil. Add the minced shallot,
remaining minced garlic, and Italian season-
ing. Cook until fragrant, 2 to 3 minutes. Add the
tomatoes, ¼ tsp salt, and a pinch of pepper, and
reduce heat to medium-low. Simmer until slightly
thickened, 5 to 7 minutes.
Add 1¼ cups water and a pinch of salt to a small
saucepan and bring to a boil over high heat.
Add the Israeli couscous, reduce heat to low,
cover, and cook until water is absorbed, about
10 minutes. Add the lemon zest, lemon juice, just
half the torn basil, 2 tsp olive oil, and a pinch
of salt and pepper. Stir the lemon basil couscous
to combine.
5. Remove the baked eggplant from the oven
and top with garlicky tomato sauce and
breadcrumb mixture. Return to the oven and
bake until breadcrumbs are golden brown,
4 to 6 minutes.
6. Divide the lemon basil couscous between bowls
and top with eggplant parmesan. Sprinkle with
the remaining torn basil and serve with lemon
wedges. Tuck in!

Time for Kraut
Sauerkraut is experiencing a bit of a renaissance.
Made from fermented cabbage and spices, it has a
slightly sour taste which makes it the perfect foil
for foods like sweet potatoes. But kraut is more
han a tasty condiment: It has numerous health
benefits, too! It's chock-full of healthy probiotics,
fiber, and iron, plus it has a significant amount of
Vitamin A.
